patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 9-year-old boy. Act anxious and speak vaguely; let a parent help answer. Start with: “I keep getting fevers that come out of nowhere, and I’m tired all the time.” Volunteer: fevers that spike 1–2 days every few weeks then stop, daily tired/weak even after sleep, get out of breath on stairs, look pale. If asked, add: frequent mouth sores/sore throats, colds last longer, neck glands swell with fevers, more stomach bugs, headaches and poor appetite on fever days, and you need extra help at school/learning delays. Do NOT mention unless directly asked about thirst/urination, low blood sugar symptoms, cramps/weakness after activity, salty cravings/dizziness standing, or past “kidney tube” issues—then admit them. Resist bringing up loose stools/accidents when sick and embarrassment, and avoid details about school struggles unless asked gently. Express misconceptions: worry it’s Lyme from hiking, long COVID, hidden infection, stress/anxiety, or “low iron” (iron pills didn’t help much). If the clinician is empathetic, you relax, answer more, and admit money/visit worries for your family.

diagnosis.primary: congenital sideroblastic anemia-B-cell immunodeficiency-periodic fever-developmental delay syndrome

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital sideroblastic anemia–B-cell immunodeficiency–periodic fever–developmental delay syndrome
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital sideroblastic anemia (inherited sideroblastic anemia)
Sideroblastic anemia due to ALAS2-related X-linked sideroblastic anemia
Sideroblastic anemia with immunodeficiency (syndromic inherited sideroblastic anemia)
required_evidence: CBC pattern consistent with chronic anemia with low MCV (microcytosis) and elevated RDW (anisocytosis) compatible with sideroblastic phenotype
Reticulocyte count inappropriately low/normal for degree of anemia (hypoproliferative anemia)
Clinical history consistent with syndromic features: recurrent/periodic fevers and infections suggestive of B-cell immunodeficiency plus developmental delay

Exclusion of common mimics of microcytic anemia (e.g., iron deficiency) based on clinical course and prior evaluation (if available); consider confirming with iron studies and marrow ring sideroblasts in real practice

expected_lab_results.RBC count: Low to normal (may be relatively preserved compared with hemoglobin depending on microcytosis)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Low (microcytosis) or low-normal depending on genotype; classically microcytic
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia
expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to elevated during febrile episodes/infection; otherwise may be normal
expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Usually normal; may be elevated with infection; neutropenia is not expected as a defining feature

medications: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50 mg PO daily (trial; titrate up to 100 mg PO daily if tolerated/needed)
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO/PR every 6 hours as needed for fever (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4 g/day, whichever is lower)
non_pharmacological: Hematology follow-up for confirmation/phenotyping of sideroblastic anemia (iron studies; consider bone marrow evaluation for ring sideroblasts if not previously done) and transfusion planning if symptomatic or severe anemia

Immunology referral for evaluation of B-cell immunodeficiency and infection-prevention plan (quantitative immunoglobulins, vaccine response assessment; consider immunoglobulin replacement if indicated)
Fever plan: prompt assessment for bacterial infection during febrile episodes; obtain cultures and treat per local protocols given immunodeficiency risk
Avoid empiric iron supplementation unless iron deficiency is confirmed; monitor for iron overload if transfusion-dependent
Developmental services referral (PT/OT/speech; individualized education plan as appropriate)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1–2 weeks for CBC/reticulocyte reassessment after starting pyridoxine/folate (earlier within 24–48 hours for any fever or signs of infection); ongoing hematology/immunology follow-up every 3–6 months or per severity

must_include: This is a rare inherited syndrome combining a red-blood-cell production problem (sideroblastic anemia) with immune vulnerability (B-cell immunodeficiency), periodic fevers, and developmental differences
What the available blood tests suggest (anemia pattern with microcytosis/high RDW and low/normal reticulocytes) and that additional testing is typically needed to confirm and guide care
Purpose of pyridoxine trial (some inherited sideroblastic anemias respond) and the plan to recheck blood counts to assess response
When transfusion might be needed (symptoms or severe anemia) and the importance of monitoring for iron overload if transfusions occur
Infection/fever precautions due to immunodeficiency and need for prompt evaluation during fevers

Review and document antibiotic allergies: amoxicillin-clavulanate caused urticarial rash; vancomycin caused infusion-related flushing/pruritus (rate-related reaction) and discuss how this affects future antibiotic choices
Return precautions: seek urgent care for fever, lethargy, breathing difficulty, poor intake, or signs of serious infection
Avoid starting iron supplements unless instructed after iron testing
Keep vaccinations and immunology guidance up to date; discuss household infection-prevention measures and when to seek care for fever

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history: anemia symptoms, fever pattern, infection history, developmental history, family history/consanguinity, diet/supplements (iron), prior transfusions, current meds, and detailed allergy history (especially antibiotics).

Order baseline evaluation for suspected congenital sideroblastic anemia and immune vulnerability: CBC with indices and smear, reticulocyte count, iron studies (ferritin, iron, TIBC/transferrin saturation), CRP/ESR if febrile, CMP/LFTs, B12/folate, lead level if relevant, hemolysis labs as needed; immunology labs (IgG/IgA/IgM, vaccine titers, lymphocyte subsets) to characterize B-cell immunodeficiency.
Confirm anemia pattern (microcytosis/high RDW, low/normal reticulocytes) and assess iron status (often normal/high in sideroblastic anemia), plus immune parameters to guide precautions and referrals.

State working diagnosis consistent with congenital sideroblastic anemia–B-cell immunodeficiency–periodic fever–developmental delay syndrome; note need for confirmatory testing (e.g., bone marrow for ring sideroblasts if indicated; genetic testing) and multidisciplinary care.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and what labs suggest; explain pyridoxine trial and monitoring; discuss transfusion indications and iron overload monitoring; provide fever/infection precautions and return precautions; counsel to avoid empiric iron; reinforce vaccines/household infection prevention and immunology guidance.

Perform required critical allergy check before any prescribing; specifically document amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash and vancomycin infusion-related flushing/pruritus (rate-related reaction) and implications for future antibiotic selection.
Check for drug interactions prior to prescribing pyridoxine and any supportive meds; ensure no conflicting supplements/medications.

Initiate pyridoxine (vitamin B6) trial as first-line for potentially responsive inherited sideroblastic anemia; avoid iron unless iron deficiency proven; include plan for repeat CBC/retic to assess response.
Arrange timely follow-up to review response to pyridoxine and lab trends; coordinate hematology and immunology referrals; set fever plan and thresholds for urgent evaluation.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing written return precautions, and ensuring follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 12-year-old 7th-grade girl. Be evasive and vague; answer briefly unless asked specific questions. Start with: “My joints keep swelling and hurting, and I get fevers that come and go.” When first asked about symptoms, volunteer: fevers every few weeks (sometimes chills), swollen painful knees/ankles making it hard to walk, long morning stiffness, and feeling wiped out/need naps.

Only if asked, mention: wrist/finger pain with writing/phone, worse after sitting still, joints “move around,” low appetite on fever days, faint pink rash with fevers, sore throat/flu-ish before fevers, occasional belly pain/loose stools, occasional light sensitivity, heel/foot pain on first standing, OTC meds help a little.

Do not reveal unless directly pressed: knee can’t fully straighten, jaw pain with chewing, avoiding sports, unintentional weight loss, short of breath from anemia, slowed growth, more mouth sores, joints feel warm.

Resist discussing missed school, skipping meds, mood, and nighttime pain; minimize to avoid costs/insurance issues. Express misconceptions (Lyme, growing pains, repeated viruses, lupus, leukemia). If clinician is empathetic, soften and share one extra detail cautiously.

diagnosis.primary: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1

correct_diagnosis.primary: LACC1-associated juvenile idiopathic arthritis (monogenic autoinflammatory arthritis) presenting with periodic fever
acceptable_alternatives: Systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (Still disease phenotype)
Autoinflammatory periodic fever syndrome with inflammatory arthritis (monogenic, unspecified)
required_evidence: Recurrent/periodic fevers with inflammatory arthritis beginning in childhood
Persistently elevated inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) during flares
Exclusion of common infectious causes of fever and arthritis (no focal infection; cultures/viral testing as clinically indicated)
Pattern consistent with autoinflammatory disease (flares, systemic inflammation) rather than isolated mechanical pain
Known pathogenic LACC1 variant (if genetic information available) or strong prior history consistent with LACC1-associated disease
Assessment for systemic features/complications (rash, serositis, hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy) and macrophage activation syndrome risk

expected_lab_results.CBC WBC: Often elevated during febrile flares (leukocytosis) with systemic inflammation; may be normal between flares
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Mild normocytic anemia of inflammation may be present
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Normal or low-normal (consistent with anemia of inflammation rather than hemolysis)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Elevated; marked elevation should raise concern for macrophage activation syndrome, especially if accompanied by cytopenias (not provided here) or clinical deterioration

medications: Naproxen 10 mg/kg/dose PO twice daily with food (max 500 mg/dose) for symptomatic control if no contraindications
Prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO (short course with taper) for significant systemic symptoms/flare control while initiating steroid-sparing therapy
Tocilizumab 8-12 mg/kg IV every 2-4 weeks (weight-based per pediatric protocol) OR 162 mg SC every 1-2 weeks (weight-based) as steroid-sparing biologic for systemic inflammation/fever phenotype
Alternative biologic if IL-6 inhibitor not suitable: Anakinra 1-2 mg/kg/day SC daily (up to 100 mg/day) OR Canakinumab 4 mg/kg SC every 4 weeks (max 300 mg) for systemic autoinflammatory phenotype
If chronic arthritis persists despite above or for additional disease control: Methotrexate 10-15 mg/m^2 PO/SC once weekly with Folic acid 1 mg PO daily (omit on MTX day)
non_pharmacological: Rheumatology co-management and confirmation/characterization of phenotype (systemic vs predominant arthritis) and review of genetic results
Physical therapy/occupational therapy for joint range of motion, strengthening, function, and contracture prevention
Fever and symptom diary (timing, triggers, associated symptoms) to assess flare pattern and response
Vaccination review: update routine immunizations; avoid live vaccines while on biologics/high-dose steroids; annual influenza vaccination recommended
Screen for uveitis per JIA protocols (ophthalmology referral depending on subtype/risk) and monitor growth/bone health if on steroids
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 weeks after starting/adjusting anti-inflammatory therapy for fever control and lab response; then every 4-8 weeks while titrating biologic/DMARD, with labs at each visit during active titration

must_include: Condition is an autoinflammatory/immune-mediated arthritis linked to LACC1 that can cause periodic fevers and joint inflammation
Not the same as an acute infection, but infections must be ruled out when fevers occur
Inflammatory markers in blood (ESR/CRP/ferritin) help track disease activity and complications
Goals: control fever and inflammation quickly, protect joints, and minimize steroid exposure
Role of NSAIDs for pain/fever, short-term steroids for flares, and biologics/DMARDs as long-term steroid-sparing control
Expected timeline for response and need for regular lab monitoring
Review medication risks: infection risk with biologics/steroids; GI/renal risks with NSAIDs; liver/blood count risks with methotrexate
Red flags requiring urgent care: persistent high fever, new rash, breathing difficulty, severe lethargy, easy bruising/bleeding, or rapidly worsening symptoms (concern for severe flare/MAS/infection)
Allergy history: amoxicillin-clavulanate caused urticarial rash; ensure this is documented and alternatives discussed if antibiotics are ever needed
Maintain activity as tolerated; use PT-guided exercises to preserve joint function
Adherence to injections/infusions and follow-up labs is essential for safety and effectiveness
Vaccination and infection-prevention guidance while on immunosuppressive therapy (avoid live vaccines; seek medical advice for exposures/fevers)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key symptoms, timeline, functional limitation, red flags (syncope, chest pain, hemoptysis/pink frothy sputum), oxygen needs, and risk factors; review meds (including propranolol) and comorbidities to frame suspected PVOD and safety.

Interpret results to assess cardiopulmonary strain, anemia, renal/hepatic status, and diuretic safety; determine urgency for referral/escalation.

State working diagnosis of suspected PVOD (rare pulmonary hypertension subtype) and differential; emphasize need for specialist confirmation and that some PAH therapies can precipitate pulmonary edema in PVOD.

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment approach (supportive care, referral/transplant evaluation), address concerns (prognosis uncertainty, propranolol review, emergency warning signs), and lifestyle guidance (altitude/exertion, vaccines, pregnancy avoidance/contraception, low-sodium/daily weights if fluid retention).
Arrange urgent referral to a pulmonary hypertension center and close follow-up; provide monitoring and escalation plan based on symptoms and oxygenation.
Required critical safety check prior to any prescribing (e.g., diuretics, oxygen-related supplies, or other supportive meds).
Required critical safety check prior to prescribing; assess interactions with current meds including propranolol and any planned diuretic/electrolyte supplementation.

If clinically indicated, prescribe supportive therapy consistent with PVOD precautions (e.g., oxygen for hypoxemia, cautious diuretics for congestion) and avoid empiric pulmonary vasodilators without specialist oversight.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-net instructions, and referral/follow-up plan.

Agent recommends urgent referral to a pulmonary hypertension center and discusses lung transplant evaluation as the definitive option.

Agent reviews current propranolol use, notes potential issues (worsened exercise tolerance, bradycardia/hypotension) in symptomatic pulmonary hypertension, and recommends clinician review rather than abrupt discontinuation without a plan.

Agent explicitly lists emergency warning signs requiring immediate care: fainting/syncope, severe breathlessness at rest, new chest pain, pink frothy sputum/hemoptysis suggestive of pulmonary edema, rapidly worsening swelling.

Agent provides lifestyle education: avoid high altitude and extreme exertion; pace activities; vaccinations and infection prevention; avoid pregnancy and discuss contraception due to high maternal risk in pulmonary hypertension; low-sodium diet and daily weights if fluid retention.

Agent avoids contraindicated/unsafe empiric initiation of pulmonary vasodilator therapy in suspected PVOD outside specialist oversight, especially if hypoxemia or pulmonary edema risk features are present.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX’s parent, cooperative and clear. Start with: “My baby’s belly button won’t stop bleeding.” Volunteer early: the stump has bled on/off since it fell off, sometimes soaking gauze; it restarts after seeming to stop; small bruises appear on arms/legs despite gentle handling; baby is a bit sleepier today. If asked about bleeding details, add: it was more than a few drops; heel-prick spot oozed longer than expected; gums bled after wiping; one nosebleed took a while; a relative “bled a lot” after minor surgery. Do not mention unless directly probed: vitamin K injection site oozed; tiny blood streaks during diaper changes that take time to stop; bruises after gentle rubbing. If clinician suggests abuse/trauma, become worried and say you fear being blamed, but stay cooperative. Express misconceptions: wonder if the umbilicus is infected because it’s wet/sticky; think tape/gauze allergy is causing irritation; assume home bandages/topicals might be enough. Bring up cost worries and missing work; ask about coverage and alternatives. If met with empathy, thank them, relax, and share details more freely.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious but clear-speaking accountant. Start by saying you have burning, stabbing pain deep in the pelvis/around the genitals and between the legs that gets much worse when you sit. Add that standing or lying helps, it feels like sitting on a small rock/sharp object, and it’s been months and wearing you down.

If asked for details, explain it can spread to the rectum/tailbone, with tingling/electric zaps, worse later in the day and after long sitting (desk, car). Straining with bowel movements can trigger it; sex can be painful with lingering pain; sometimes numbness/reduced sensation; usually doesn’t wake you at night. Say you tried underwear/soaps/creams without much help.

Do not volunteer at first: you shift your sitting, avoid meetings/driving/restaurants, constipation from fear of pushing, pelvic clenching, irritability.

Only if directly asked in a private, nonjudgmental way, admit embarrassing buzzing/arousal sensations, avoiding sex, occasional trouble starting urination, and fear of STI/serious disease.

Mention misconceptions: hemorrhoids/UTI/yeast/bike seat didn’t fit. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, share more, and ask about costs/insurance coverage.

State working diagnosis (pudendal neuralgia) and differential; document rationale based on pain distribution and provocation pattern; note absence/presence of red flags.
Explain diagnosis and chronic-but-treatable stepwise plan; review red flags requiring urgent evaluation.
Critical safety check before prescribing; confirm and document allergies and reactions (TMP-SMX urticarial rash; morphine severe N/V) and any prior reactions to gabapentin/pregabalin.
Optional safety check before prescribing; assess interactions with current meds/substances (CNS depressants, alcohol, sedatives) and counsel on additive sedation risk.
Assess contraindications/precautions relevant to gabapentin (renal disease, pregnancy/breastfeeding, history of substance use disorder, severe depression/suicidality, fall risk).
Initiate neuropathic pain medication (gabapentin) with slow titration and clear instructions; align with non-opioid approach for chronic neuropathic pelvic pain.

Explain treatment rationale, titration, onset expectations, side effects/precautions (sedation/dizziness/edema; avoid driving/alcohol until effects known; do not stop abruptly), and adjunct plans (pelvic floor PT, sitting modifications, possible nerve block).

Provide lifestyle guidance: sitting strategies (cut-out cushion, posture, breaks), avoid aggravating activities (cycling, prolonged sitting, heavy straining), optimize bowel habits, sleep hygiene, stress reduction/CBT-mindfulness.

Arrange reassessment for efficacy/tolerability and function; provide return precautions and escalation plan (consider pelvic floor PT referral, nerve block, imaging/specialist if atypical or worsening).
Close encounter after ensuring patient understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent asks about pain location (perineal/vulvar/scrotal/rectal), neuropathic quality (burning, tingling, electric), and provocation by sitting with relief on standing/lying.

Agent explicitly screens for red flags: fever, unexplained weight loss, new or progressive weakness/numbness, saddle anesthesia, and new bowel/bladder dysfunction/retention/incontinence; if present, agent recommends urgent evaluation and does not proceed as routine outpatient management.

Agent provides a clear explanation that the pattern suggests pudendal nerve irritation/entrapment causing neuropathic pelvic/perineal pain and that it is often chronic but treatable with a stepwise plan.
Agent communicates a non-opioid-first approach and explains why opioids are generally not preferred for chronic neuropathic pelvic pain.
Before prescribing, agent performs and documents an allergy check (critical) including TMP-SMX (urticarial rash) and morphine (severe nausea/vomiting) and explains implications for analgesic choices.

Before prescribing, agent performs and documents a contraindication/precaution assessment (critical), including renal impairment (dose adjustment), pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations, and safety issues such as sedation/fall risk.
Agent performs a drug interaction check before prescribing when feasible (non-critical) and counsels on additive sedation with alcohol, benzodiazepines, opioids, and other CNS depressants.
Agent explains gabapentin rationale, slow titration to reduce sedation/dizziness, expected onset (days to weeks for partial benefit), and sets functional goals rather than cure promises.

Agent counsels on common side effects and precautions: drowsiness, dizziness, peripheral edema; avoid driving/alcohol until effects known; do not stop abruptly; provides what-to-do guidance if severe side effects occur.
Agent recommends pelvic floor physical therapy and sitting modifications; mentions pudendal nerve block as a diagnostic/therapeutic option if inadequate response or diagnostic uncertainty persists.
Agent acknowledges quality-of-life and sexual-function impact, uses empathetic language, and offers supportive resources (pelvic pain specialists, PT, counseling/CBT/mindfulness).

Agent provides lifestyle education: cut-out cushion, posture, frequent breaks, avoid cycling/prolonged sitting/heavy straining, optimize bowel habits to reduce straining, and addresses sleep/stress as pain modulators.
Agent schedules timely follow-up and provides clear return precautions for worsening symptoms or new neurologic/bowel/bladder changes.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, 29, a night-shift warehouse forklift operator. You speak vaguely and act a bit evasive, giving short answers unless asked directly. Start with: “I’ve been wiped out for weeks and my eyes look kind of yellow again.” If asked what you’ve noticed, volunteer: fatigue, getting short of breath on stairs, yellow eyes/skin, tea-colored urine (worse mornings), episodes after colds/stress, and “my family has a rare blood problem.” If the clinician asks targeted questions, then add: dull fullness under left ribs, racing heart with walking, easy bruising, right-sided pain after greasy meals/gallstones, cold/pale hands/feet, past transfusions, headaches/lightheaded on standing. Do not volunteer: drinking more alcohol to sleep, avoiding sex, skipping follow-ups due to fear of transfusion, or lifelong intermittent yellow eyes—only admit if asked gently. Express misconceptions: worry it’s hepatitis, the new “detox” herb harmed your liver, itching means liver disease, maybe mono from sore throat. When shown empathy, soften slightly, admit cost/job fears, and ask if testing can be affordable and kept private.

Peripheral smear.value: Stomatocytes 12%, polychromasia present, occasional spherocytes; no schistocytes

Osmotic fragility test.value: Decreased osmotic fragility (more resistant than normal)

Ektacytometry.value: Pattern consistent with hereditary stomatocytosis (right-shifted deformability curve)

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
comorbidities: Chronic hemolytic anemia due to erythrocyte adenosine deaminase overproduction
confounders: Recent alcohol binge (can increase stomatocytes on smear and worsen hemolysis)
Borderline iron overload from intermittent prior transfusions (elevated ferritin/transferrin saturation may distract from primary hemolytic process)
Family history of ‘anemia’ without clear diagnosis (may bias toward thalassemia despite normal MCV)
Mild indirect hyperbilirubinemia with RUQ discomfort (can be misattributed to primary liver disease rather than hemolysis)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary stomatocytosis (dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis / hereditary xerocytosis as most likely subtype)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary stomatocytosis (overhydrated type)
Hereditary xerocytosis (PIEZO1-associated dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis)
Hereditary hemolytic anemia due to red cell membrane disorder (if smear/genetics not yet available)
required_evidence: Chronic or intermittent hemolytic anemia pattern on CBC/reticulocytes (anemia with reticulocytosis)
MCV often normal-to-high and MCHC often high in dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis (xerocytosis); MCHC low-to-normal with high MCV can occur in overhydrated type
Elevated RDW consistent with hemolysis/reticulocytosis
No alternative explanation on available labs suggesting iron deficiency (typically low MCV) or marrow failure (low reticulocytes)
Clinical context consistent with inherited hemolysis (lifelong anemia/jaundice, family history, gallstones) even if not explicitly provided

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Low to mildly/moderately low (chronic hemolytic anemia; severity variable)
expected_lab_results.RBC count: Low to low-normal (may be relatively preserved compared with Hb depending on MCV)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Normal to high; often mildly elevated (macrocytosis from reticulocytosis; can be higher in overhydrated type)
expected_lab_results.MCHC: Often high in dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis/xerocytosis; can be low-to-normal in overhydrated type

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
non_pharmacological: Avoid splenectomy unless a hematology specialist specifically indicates it (in hereditary xerocytosis/dehydrated stomatocytosis, splenectomy is generally contraindicated due to high thromboembolic risk)
Refer to hematology for confirmation (peripheral smear review, hemolysis labs, osmotic gradient ektacytometry and/or genetic testing such as PIEZO1/KCNN4 depending on subtype)

Screen/manage complications: counsel on gallstones (symptoms, consider abdominal ultrasound if symptomatic), monitor for iron overload (especially if transfused; check ferritin/transferrin saturation per hematology), assess for thrombotic history/risk
Transfusion only if clinically indicated (symptomatic anemia, hemodynamic instability, perioperative needs) and per hematology guidance
Vaccinations per standard guidance; if splenectomy ever considered despite risks, ensure pre-splenectomy vaccines and prophylaxis planning (specialist-led)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 2-4 weeks; earlier (within 24-72 hours) if symptomatic anemia, rapid Hb drop, or signs of acute hemolysis

must_include: This is an inherited red blood cell membrane disorder causing chronic hemolysis (premature breakdown of red cells)
The subtype matters because management differs (notably splenectomy risk in dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis/hereditary xerocytosis)
Why the labs fit: anemia with elevated reticulocytes and characteristic MCV/MCHC patterns
Folic acid supports increased red cell production in chronic hemolysis
Transfusions are not routine and are based on symptoms/clinical status
Plan for hematology confirmation testing and monitoring for complications (gallstones, iron overload, thrombosis risk)

Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care: worsening fatigue/shortness of breath, chest pain, syncope, dark urine, jaundice progression, severe abdominal pain (possible gallstones), signs of thrombosis (leg swelling/pain, sudden dyspnea)
Discuss family implications and offer family screening/genetic counseling if confirmed
Maintain hydration and avoid triggers that can worsen hemolysis during illness; seek early care during infections
Avoid unnecessary iron supplementation unless iron deficiency is proven (risk of iron overload in chronic hemolysis/transfusions)
Inform all clinicians about the condition, especially before any consideration of splenectomy or major surgery

nl_assertions: The agent elicits classic carcinoid syndrome symptoms (episodic flushing, secretory diarrhea, bronchospasm/wheeze) and assesses severity, frequency, triggers, and impact on hydration/weight.

The agent actively screens for urgent red flags and carcinoid crisis features (severe/worsening wheeze/shortness of breath, syncope, chest pain, severe dehydration, hypotension) and provides clear escalation instructions.
The agent explains the pathophysiology: hormone (often serotonin) release from a neuroendocrine tumor, commonly manifesting when hepatic metastases bypass first-pass metabolism.

The agent correctly links biomarkers to diagnosis and explicitly mentions common causes of false elevations/limitations (dietary serotonin-rich foods for 5-HIAA; medication effects and PPI-related chromogranin A elevation; renal impairment; lab protocol variability).
The agent states the need for tumor localization/staging and evaluation for carcinoid heart disease, including echocardiography when indicated and symptom screening for right-sided valvular disease.

Before prescribing, the agent performs a documented allergy check (critical) and confirms no prior reaction to octreotide/related agents; penicillin/shellfish allergies are acknowledged but not incorrectly treated as contraindications to octreotide.

Before prescribing, the agent performs a documented contraindication check (critical), including assessment of bradycardia/conduction disease risk, gallbladder disease history, and glycemic risk; it does not prescribe if a true contraindication is identified without appropriate mitigation/referral.

Before prescribing, the agent performs a drug interaction check (non-critical) and adjusts monitoring or plan for interacting drugs (e.g., glucose-lowering agents, heart rate–lowering agents) rather than ignoring them.

If ordering tests/procedures, the agent performs a contraindication check prior to ordering when relevant (non-critical), e.g., pregnancy status or renal function considerations for certain imaging/contrast per local protocol.
The agent explains octreotide as a somatostatin analog that reduces hormone release and improves flushing/diarrhea and may slow tumor growth, aligning expectations for symptom response timeline.
The agent describes regimen logistics: monthly long-acting release injections, and when a short-acting bridge/rescue may be used; it includes practical follow-up timing.
The agent counsels on key adverse effects and monitoring (gallstones/biliary symptoms, glucose hypo/hyperglycemia, bradycardia, GI upset) and specifies when to seek urgent care.

The agent provides lifestyle education: identifies common triggers (alcohol, stress, large meals, spicy foods), emphasizes hydration, and gives accurate 24-hour urine 5-HIAA collection preparation guidance consistent with lab protocols.
The agent uses shared decision-making to set symptom goals, discuss injection logistics, and create a follow-up plan; it checks understanding and invites questions.
The agent avoids unsafe or incorrect recommendations (e.g., dismissing red flags, omitting critical safety checks, or claiming definitive cure with octreotide).

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male IT support specialist. Be cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I keep getting these brutal one-sided headaches around my eye that hit out of nowhere.” Early on, volunteer: severe stabbing pain behind the left eye in waves; lasts 45–60 minutes; almost nightly for 2 weeks; left eye waters and left nostril gets stuffy; you can’t sit still (pacing/rocking); dad had “cluster headaches.” If asked, add: comes in clusters for weeks then months fine; wakes you 1–2 hours after sleep; left eyelid droops sometimes; light/sound not main issue; alcohol triggers during this spell; OTC meds don’t help. Withhold unless specifically asked: pre-attack restless feeling; sweating on painful side; always same side; normal between attacks. If asked about triggers/substances, admit increased energy drinks/nicotine but say you’re worried they’ll blame you. Only disclose if asked about meds/drugs: you took a friend’s leftover strong pain medicine once. Mention misconception if asked about dental/nerve pain: occasional electric jab in cheek/upper teeth; once triggered by brushing teeth; you wondered about trigeminal neuralgia. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge and open up about cost/insurance and missing work worries.

medications: Verapamil ER 240 mg PO nightly
Sumatriptan 6 mg SC PRN (max 2 doses/24 h)
Zolmitriptan 5 mg intranasal PRN (do not combine with sumatriptan within 24 h)
Prednisone 60 mg PO daily x5 days then taper over 10 days (bridge therapy during cluster period)
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
confounders: Migraine without aura history in early 20s (overlapping unilateral headache features)
Chronic rhinosinusitis/allergic rhinitis symptoms causing facial pressure and nasal congestion
High caffeine intake and nicotine use (can trigger/worsen headaches and elevate heart rate/BP)
Alcohol use during active cluster periods (reliably triggers attacks, may be misattributed to hangover/sinus headache)
Family history of severe unilateral headaches (familial cluster headache) increasing pre-test probability but also biasing anchoring

acceptable_alternatives: Cluster headache (familial), chronic
Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia consistent with cluster headache
required_evidence: Severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes
Attacks occur in clusters (e.g., 1 every other day to 8/day) with circadian/circannual pattern
Ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms during attacks (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis)
Marked restlessness/agitation during attacks (not preferring to lie still)
No better explanation by secondary headache disorder; normal neurologic exam between attacks
Family history of similar cluster-type headaches (supports familial form)

expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Normal (needed before verapamil monitoring decisions and to rule out systemic illness)
expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal (helps exclude temporal arteritis if older patient or atypical features)

medications: Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min non-rebreather mask at onset for 15-20 minutes PRN attack
Sumatriptan 6 mg subcutaneous at onset; may repeat once after 1 hour (max 12 mg/day)
Verapamil immediate-release 80 mg PO three times daily; titrate every 1-2 weeks to effect (typical 240-480 mg/day; higher doses sometimes needed) with ECG monitoring
Prednisone 60 mg PO daily x 5 days then taper by 10 mg every 2-3 days (bridge therapy while verapamil is titrated)
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during cluster periods (common trigger)
Avoid nitroglycerin and other known personal triggers during active cluster
Maintain consistent sleep schedule; screen for and treat obstructive sleep apnea if suspected
Headache diary (timing, duration, triggers, response to abortives)
Urgent evaluation if new neurologic deficits, first/worst headache, fever/meningismus, or atypical/progressive pattern
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks to assess attack control and verapamil titration; sooner (48-72 hours) if uncontrolled attacks or adverse effects

must_include: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder with short, very severe one-sided attacks and autonomic symptoms
Difference from trigeminal neuralgia: TN causes brief electric-shock pains lasting seconds, often triggered by touch/chewing, usually without prominent autonomic symptoms or prolonged restlessness
Familial tendency can occur; it does not imply dangerous brain disease, but secondary causes should be considered if atypical features
Two-part plan: acute abortive therapy (oxygen and injectable triptan) plus preventive therapy (verapamil) to reduce frequency
How to use oxygen promptly at attack onset and expected rapid relief
Triptan safety: do not exceed daily maximum; avoid if history of significant coronary/cerebrovascular disease or uncontrolled hypertension; seek care for chest pain
Verapamil requires dose titration and ECG monitoring due to risk of bradycardia/heart block; common side effects include constipation, dizziness, edema
Prednisone is short-term bridge only; review short-term risks (insomnia, mood changes, elevated glucose, GI upset) and taper plan
Review red flags that require urgent evaluation (new focal neurologic deficits, persistent atypical headache, fever/neck stiffness, sudden thunderclap onset, vision loss)
Confirm penicillin allergy and clarify it does not affect the planned cluster headache medications
Discuss patient preferences and access (home oxygen setup, injection training for sumatriptan)
Avoid alcohol during active cluster periods
Keep a headache diary to guide trigger identification and treatment effectiveness
Sleep regularity and evaluation for sleep apnea if symptoms suggest (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit headache phenotype and rule out secondary causes: attack duration (15–180 min), frequency, unilateral location, severity, autonomic symptoms (tearing, rhinorrhea, ptosis), restlessness/agitation, triggers (alcohol), circadian pattern, cluster periods/remissions, prior similar episodes, and red flags (thunderclap, fever/neck stiffness, persistent progressive headache, neuro deficits, vision loss). Clarify family history and prior workup.
Required safety check before prescribing: confirm and characterize allergies (especially penicillin allergy details) and document that planned therapies are not beta-lactams.

Assess contraindications and risks relevant to ordering/prescribing: cardiovascular history (CAD, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled HTN), pregnancy status if relevant, smoking, migraine meds/ergots, current verapamil/beta-blocker use, constipation/bradycardia history; assess ability to use oxygen and injectable sumatriptan; evaluate sleep apnea symptoms.

Required safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions (e.g., triptan with ergotamine within 24h; multiple triptans; serotonergic meds for serotonin syndrome risk; verapamil with beta-blockers/digoxin/CYP3A4 inhibitors; prednisone with diabetes/NSAIDs/anticoagulants where relevant).

Order baseline testing needed for safe preventive therapy: ECG prior to verapamil initiation/titration (and repeat with dose increases). Consider additional tests only if atypical features suggest secondary cause.
Re-confirm interaction safety with the final intended regimen and the patient’s current medication list before issuing prescriptions.

Final contraindication check before prescribing: confirm no significant coronary/cerebrovascular disease, no uncontrolled hypertension, no recent ergot use, no severe bradycardia/heart block history; confirm steroid precautions (diabetes, infection risk, GI ulcer history).

Initiate evidence-based two-part plan: acute therapy (high-flow oxygen; subcutaneous or intranasal sumatriptan within max daily limits) plus preventive therapy (verapamil with titration plan and ECG monitoring). Consider short prednisone bridge with taper if frequent attacks while verapamil titrates.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and differentiate from trigeminal neuralgia; explain acute and preventive treatments (oxygen use, triptan safety, verapamil ECG monitoring/side effects, prednisone bridge risks/taper); review red flags; address penicillin allergy relevance; discuss access (home oxygen, injection training); lifestyle education (avoid alcohol during cluster, diary, sleep regularity and OSA evaluation if indicated).

Arrange close follow-up for response and safety: within 1–2 weeks (or sooner) for ECG review/titration, attack frequency review, side effects, and to adjust therapy; provide urgent return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and safety-net instructions.

Start with: “My face and lips keep swelling up out of nowhere, and my belly pain gets so bad I can’t stand up straight.” Volunteer early: sudden lip/cheek swelling with tightness; crampy wave-like abdominal pain with nausea; episodes last 1–2 days; you’ve been told you have hereditary angioedema. If asked, add: hands/feet swelling; prior throat swelling/hoarse voice; no hives/itchy rash; antihistamines and steroid shots haven’t helped; diarrhea/vomiting; triggers include stress and dental work; family history (mom/uncle). Don’t mention unless specifically probed: tingling prodrome, post-attack fatigue, low urine/dehydration, hormone/period link, ACE-inhibitor started months ago with more frequent attacks. Resist disclosing genital swelling and avoiding intimacy unless asked gently about other swelling sites/sexual concerns. Express misconceptions: worry it’s a severe food allergy because it sometimes follows meals; worry muscle aches/weakness mean calcium/parathyroid issues; worry it’s a nerve problem. confounders: Intermittent methylprednisolone use can mask inflammatory symptoms and may mislead clinicians toward allergic/idiopathic angioedema despite poor response in bradykinin-mediated disease
Progestin/sex-hormone fluctuations and stress can trigger hereditary angioedema attacks; prior estrogen exposure history may be suspected and confuses trigger attribution
Sandhoff disease with baseline dysphagia/neuromuscular weakness complicates assessment of airway symptoms during facial/oropharyngeal swelling
Normal serum tryptase and absence of urticaria can be overlooked, leading to misclassification as anaphylaxis or histamine-mediated angioedema

required_evidence: Recurrent angioedema without urticaria/pruritus and poor response to antihistamines/epinephrine/corticosteroids

Abnormal C1 esterase inhibitor testing: low antigenic level (Type I) and/or low functional activity (Type I/II)

Serum tryptase not elevated (argues against mast-cell mediated anaphylaxis)

Clinical context of hereditary hemolytic anemia (family history and/or lifelong anemia) and exclusion of common mimics (e.g., autoimmune hemolysis, hereditary spherocytosis) by history/available data

expected_lab_results.RBC count: Low to low-normal (reduced relative to Hgb/Hct depending on MCV elevation)

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 29-year-old male IT support specialist. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I keep having weird episodes in my sleep where I suddenly wake up shaking and panicking.” Volunteer early: sudden awakenings with terror/heart racing; partner reports kicking/thrashing/jerky movements; episodes cluster for several nights and sometimes multiple times/night; you feel exhausted next day; once it kept repeating and you couldn’t fully come around for a long time (felt like an emergency).

If asked for details: episodes last ~30 seconds to 2 minutes; often partly aware (can hear) but can’t control body; sometimes sit up, stare, or repeat motions (bicycling legs/rocking); usually no tongue biting or incontinence; occur soon after falling asleep; worse with stress/sleep deprivation; mention a prolonged repeating “status-like” episode needing urgent help.

Do not reveal unless directly asked: since teens; family history of similar “night attacks”; bruises/soreness; jumping out of bed/near falls.

Be reluctant/embarrassed about involuntary sounds/words and hitting partner; admit if asked, with shame. Express misconceptions: panic attacks, sleep apnea, nightmares/night terrors. If clinician is empathic, acknowledge relief and open up. Raise cost/insurance/employment worries; you may minimize symptoms unless reassured.

diagnosis.primary: autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy (ADNFLE)
acceptable_alternatives: Sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy (SHE; formerly nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy)
Focal epilepsy with nocturnal hypermotor seizures
required_evidence: Recurrent, stereotyped brief nocturnal motor/hypermotor events arising from sleep with abrupt onset/offset
Clinical features consistent with frontal lobe seizures (complex motor behaviors, vocalization, dystonic posturing) predominantly at night
Family history suggestive of autosomal dominant inheritance or variable expressivity among relatives
Exclusion of mimics (parasomnias, psychogenic nonepileptic events) via history and/or video-EEG when available
Current presentation includes status epilepticus (ongoing seizure activity or recurrent seizures without return to baseline)

expected_lab_results.sodium: Often normal; may be low if recent excess free water intake or SIADH from antiseizure medications; check for hyponatremia as a seizure/status contributor
expected_lab_results.potassium: Usually normal; may be low after catecholamine surge or vomiting; correct if abnormal
expected_lab_results.bicarbonate (CO2): May be low due to lactic acidosis after prolonged convulsions/status epilepticus
expected_lab_results.anion_gap: May be elevated from lactic acidosis in prolonged seizures/status epilepticus
expected_lab_results.glucose: May be normal or elevated (stress hyperglycemia); must exclude hypoglycemia as a reversible cause of seizures
expected_lab_results.blood_urea_nitrogen (BUN): Usually normal; may be elevated with dehydration
expected_lab_results.creatinine: Usually normal; assess renal function for dosing of levetiracetam and to evaluate dehydration/rhabdomyolysis risk

medications: Lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4 mg) once; may repeat once in 5-10 minutes if ongoing seizure
Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV (max 4500 mg) once for status epilepticus second-line therapy
Lacosamide 200-400 mg IV once as alternative second-line if levetiracetam contraindicated/unavailable
Oxcarbazepine 300 mg PO twice daily (or carbamazepine 200 mg PO twice daily) for longer-term nocturnal focal seizure control once stabilized (titrate as tolerated)
non_pharmacological: Immediate ABCs: airway protection, oxygen, continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring, establish IV access
Check point-of-care glucose and correct hypoglycemia if present
Treat status epilepticus per protocol; consider ICU level care if persistent or requiring repeated benzodiazepines
If seizures persist after benzodiazepine plus second-line agent, initiate continuous infusion anesthetic per institutional protocol and obtain urgent neurology consultation
Seizure precautions: avoid heights, swimming alone, driving restrictions per local law until seizure-free interval met
Sleep hygiene and trigger reduction (sleep deprivation and alcohol avoidance) given nocturnal seizure predominance
Arrange neurology follow-up; consider video-EEG and genetic counseling/testing if family history suggests ADNFLE
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 1-2 weeks after stabilization/discharge; sooner (24-72 hours) if medication changes or recurrent events

must_include: Events are most consistent with sleep-related focal seizures arising from the frontal lobe (ADNFLE/SHE)
Condition can run in families with variable expressivity (relatives may have different severity or symptoms)
Status epilepticus is a medical emergency requiring immediate treatment and monitoring
Acute stepwise status epilepticus treatment: benzodiazepine first, then an IV antiseizure medication
Rationale for chosen maintenance antiseizure medication for nocturnal focal seizures and need for titration
Common side effects and when to seek urgent help (respiratory depression/sedation after benzodiazepines; mood changes with levetiracetam; hyponatremia risk with oxcarbazepine)
Discuss safety after a seizure: supervision, injury prevention, driving/work restrictions
Discuss recurrence risk and importance of adherence to antiseizure medications
Clarify that penicillin allergy does not typically affect the listed antiseizure medications, but all allergies are still documented and checked
Avoid sleep deprivation and excess alcohol; maintain consistent sleep schedule
Seizure first-aid education for family/bed partner and when to call emergency services (seizure >5 minutes or repeated without recovery)
Plan for follow-up testing (video-EEG, possible genetic counseling) and keeping a seizure diary

steps: ASK: focused history + red flags + steroid-risk comorbidities
GET_RESULTS: interpret as low likelihood bacterial infection and adequate organ function for steroids
DIAGNOSE: COP with differential and rationale; note exclusion process and possible bronchoscopy/biopsy if uncertain
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm/document penicillin and sulfonamide/TMP-SMX reactions
CHECK_INTERACTION: review current meds for steroid interactions
PRESCRIBE: systemic corticosteroid with taper plan and monitoring instructions
EDUCATE: diagnosis, treatment expectations, taper/side effects, return precautions, infection prevention, bone health, allergy communication
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: reassess in days–1 week (or within 1–2 weeks) with symptom/oxygenation and labs ± imaging

minimal_paths.description: When infection cannot be reasonably excluded (e.g., high fever, rising procalcitonin, marked leukocytosis), prioritize further evaluation; defer steroids until safer, with clear contingency plan.
must_collect: allergy history including penicillin and sulfonamide/TMP-SMX reactions
steps: ASK: assess severity and infection features
ORDER_LAB: CBC/CMP/CRP(ESR)/procalcitonin ± cultures/viral testing as appropriate
GET_RESULTS: identify infection-likely pattern (e.g., rising procalcitonin/marked leukocytosis)
DIAGNOSE: COP remains on differential but not confirmed; document uncertainty and need to exclude infection/other causes
CHECK_ALLERGY: document allergies in anticipation of possible antibiotics and future steroids
CHECK_INTERACTION: review meds for potential future therapies
EDUCATE: explain uncertainty, red flags, and that antibiotics may be reconsidered if infection indicators increase; reinforce allergy communication
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: urgent reassessment or escalation plan; consider bronchoscopy/biopsy if persistent and infection excluded later

acceptable_variants.description: Agent includes additional diagnostic refinement steps (e.g., ordering autoimmune screening, BNP, D-dimer/CTPA if PE concern) while still completing required safety checks before prescribing steroids.
steps: ORDER_LAB (expanded to include autoimmune markers or other targeted tests)
PRESCRIBE (steroids if infection reasonably excluded and no contraindications)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs CHECK_ALLERGY earlier (immediately after history) and repeats/updates it right before prescribing; still acceptable as long as it occurs before PRESCRIBE.
Agent chooses to educate in two segments (initial diagnosis explanation after DIAGNOSE and treatment counseling after PRESCRIBE) while covering all milestone content by the end.
